name: page-rastered-training-20260721
question: Does training on page-rastered rows (real 300 DPI page renders sliced into lines) beat training on text2image single-line images (psm 7 synthetic), so that reported eval error and real byte-restore finally agree in direction?
verdict: NO — the page-rastered model is WORSE. It fails the byte-restore gate in both cells while the psm-7 synthetic control and stock Tesseract both restore byte-identical. Recorded as a negative result; the plan's hypothesis is refuted as executed.

environment.font: courier (Nimbus Mono PS on the VM)

training.method: page-rastered: create --format pdf -> rasterize 300 DPI (pypdfium2) -> slice rows by known line geometry -> one .lstmf per row (tesseract --psm 6 lstm.train)
training.ground_truth: the PDF's own printed line list (framed: kind prefix, index token, payload, delimiter, check)

gate.definition: byte-identical restore: create -> rasterize 300 DPI -> OCR per page (whitelist = codec spec.alphabet + delimiter, --psm 6) -> extract -> byte-diff the tree

results.detail: CodecError: line L00001 failed CRC and exceeds RS correction budget
CodecError: line L00001 failed CRC and exceeds RS correction budget

why_it_failed.mechanism: INSERTIONS AND DROPS, not substitutions. On one page (75 printed lines) the page-rastered model produced 22 length-mismatched lines vs the synthetic control's 2 (and those 2 are the display-only human banner, which carries no data). Example: truth ...L3L4L3LK... read as ...L3L4AL3LK... — a spurious 'A' inserted. A length change shifts the whole line, so the fixed-width frame parse desynchronizes and the per-line CRC fails; Reed-Solomon cannot absorb it the way it absorbs an in-place substitution.
why_it_failed.likely_cause: row slicing. Training rows were cut from page rasters by computed line geometry; if a crop includes a sliver of the neighbouring row's ascenders/descenders, the model learns to emit an extra glyph for that ink. That teaches exactly the failure mode (insertion) that is most lethal to this format.
why_it_failed.note: This is a defect of THIS execution of the idea (geometry-sliced crops), not proof that page-level training is unsound. Option (b) from the plan — training directly on full page rasters with --psm 6, no slicing — was never tried and remains open.

cer_vs_restore.finding: The plan's acceptance criterion is NOT met, but for the opposite reason than feared. The original complaint was models reporting ~0% CER while failing restore. Here the page-rastered model reports a HIGHER train BCER (0.493%) than the old synthetic models ever did (~0.000-0.005%) and ALSO fails restore, while the synthetic model passes. So eval error still does not predict restore: a lower-CER model failed and a higher-CER model passed in the earlier comparison, and here the relationship inverts again.
cer_vs_restore.reinforced_lesson: Byte-restore remains the only acceptance gate. CER of any flavour — framed, unframed, page-rastered — is a proxy that can point the wrong way. Additionally: a per-glyph INSERTION/DROP rate is a better early predictor of restore than aggregate CER, because aggregate CER weights a length-changing error the same as a substitution when they are not remotely equivalent for this format.

recommendation: Do not ship the page-rastered model. Keep stock tesseract-glyphive as the base32g baseline (it restores unaided in this gate). If page-level training is revisited, try option (b) (train on whole page rasters, psm 6, no row slicing) and gate on insertion/drop rate before spending a full training run.
